answer:
\boxed{B}

In a hypothetical syllogism (e.g., "If P, then Q"), when the minor premise affirms the antecedent (e.g., "P is true"), the valid conclusion logically follows the major premise by affirming the consequent ("Therefore, Q is true"). This structure adheres to the rule of affirming the antecedent, ensuring the conclusion must affirm the consequent. Option B correctly reflects this logical progression.
